perturbation.original_text: 1.7 shall not, and shall procure that any associated company of yours shall not, employ, seek to employ, or solicit any pe rson employed by us or the Business or who holds or otherwise has access to Confid ential Information either at the date of this Agreement or at any time during negotiations for the sale of the Business or during the period of twelve months from the date of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.7 shall not, and shall procure that any associated company of yours shall not, employ, seek to employ, or solicit any person employed by us or the Business or who holds or otherwise has access to Confidential Information either at the date of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause restricts the recipient from soliciting employees for a 12-month period post-agreement. Removing the phrase 'or at any time during negotiations for the sale of the Business or during the period of twelve months from the date of this Agreement' makes the restriction far weaker, and unenforceable beyond the initial negotiation period, potentially violating state laws that aim to prevent the misuse of confidential information for competitive advantage by soliciting key employees during or shortly after failed acquisition talks.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally prohibits contracts that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. While there are exceptions, the altered clause, by only restricting solicitation at the date of agreement, could be interpreted as an unreasonable restraint if it impedes someone's ability to recruit employees who might leave the company during negotiations or within a reasonable period afterward. The original clause's 12-month restriction provides a clearer timeframe, but the modified version creates ambiguity and potential conflict with the statute if it's construed as broadly preventing future employment prospects after the date of the agreement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text omits the 12-month restriction on soliciting employees, which was present in the original text. The Perturbation Explanation highlights that this weakens the restriction, making it potentially unenforceable beyond the initial negotiation period. The Law Explanation posits that California Business and Professions Code Section 16600, as evidenced by Scraped Law Snippet 1, generally voids contracts restraining someone from engaging in a lawful profession. By removing the 12-month period, the modified clause potentially creates an unreasonable restraint if interpreted as broadly preventing future employment prospects after the agreement date. Scraped Law Snippet 1 emphasizes that section 16600(a) states 'every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void,' implying that removing the temporal limitation may create a restriction not in line with 16600's exceptions and could be considered a contradiction, impacting enforceability.

perturbation.original_text: 2.5 This agreement shall expire two years from the date of execution. Any agreement created by acceptance of this Co nfidential Information shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of (Enter State) and you hereby submit to the exclusive jurisdiction of the Courts of (Enter State) in relation thereto. If the party to this agreement is a company th e person signing this agreement on behalf of the company hereby agrees in consideration of the information being provided to the company at their request to personally guaran tee the performance of the company of its obligations hereunder which guarantee shall be a primary obligation.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.5 This agreement shall expire two years from the date of execution. Any agreement created by acceptance of this Confidential Information shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of (Enter State) and you hereby submit to the exclusive jurisdiction of the Courts of (Enter State) in relation thereto. If the party to this agreement is a company the person signing this agreement on behalf of the company hereby agrees in consideration of the information being provided to the company at their request to personally guarantee the performance of the company of its obligations hereunder.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase 'which guarantee shall be a primary obligation', the agreement no longer explicitly defines the personal guarantee as a primary obligation. This can create legal issues in states where guarantees are presumed to be secondary unless explicitly stated otherwise. This omission shifts the burden of proof and could undermine the enforceability of the guarantee against the signatory.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 2808 pertains to when a guarantor is liable to a surety.  Guarantees can be primary or secondary. Omitting 'which guarantee shall be a primary obligation' makes it less clear and more ambiguous that the guarantor is primarily, and not secondarily liable. A secondary guarantor would not be responsible until the principal defaults and creditors have pursued all means of collection against the debtor. By omitting this clause, the contract introduces risk of being interpreted under laws governing general suretyship rather than laws relating to primary obligations.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 2808 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 13. SURETYSHIP [2787 - 2856] ( Heading of Title 13 amended by Stats. 1939, Ch. 453. ) ARTICLE 4. Liability of Sureties [2806 - 2811] ( Heading of Article 4 amended by Stats. 1939, Ch. 453. ) 2808. Where one assumes liability as surety upon a conditional obligation, his liability is commensurate with that of the principal, and he is not entitled to notice of the default of the principal, unless he is unable, by the exercise of reasonable diligence, to acquire information of such default, and the creditor has actual notice thereof. (Amended by Stats. 1939, Ch. 453.)
perturbation.justification: The omission of 'which guarantee shall be a primary obligation' directly impacts the nature of the guarantee, potentially reducing it from a primary to a secondary obligation. This contradicts the intent to create a strong guarantee, which is supported by the Law Explanation. The provided law snippet, California Civil Code Section 2808, discusses liability as a surety and how it relates to the principal's obligation. While it doesn't explicitly define primary vs. secondary, the Law Explanation clarifies that omitting the phrase introduces ambiguity, potentially making the guarantor only secondarily liable. Therefore, removing the phrase weakens the legally binding nature of the guarantee, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 1.1 shall keep secret and confidential our discussions and any information about the Business supplied to you or your advisers by us or by our advisers, whether before or after this Agreement and any analyses or other documents which may be made or prepared by you which contai n or otherwise reflect such information or your review of or interest in the Business (“Confidential Information”), and not disclose to any third party any Co nfidential Information (except such information which comes into the public domain other than as a result of the disclosure by you or by your employees, agents or advisers who need to know such information for the purpose of eval uating whether or not, and on what terms, you might proceed with the proposed purchase);
perturbation.changed_text: 1.1 shall keep secret and confidential our discussions and any information about the Business supplied to you or your advisers by us or by our advisers, whether before or after this Agreement and any analyses or other documents which may be made or prepared by you which contai n or otherwise reflect such information or your review of or interest in the Business (“Confidential Information”), and not disclose to any third party any Co nfidential Information;
perturbation.explanation: The original clause has an exception that allows information to be disclosed to employees, agents, or advisers who need to know for the purpose of evaluating the purchase. Removing the exception entirely creates an absolute prohibition on disclosure, which could be deemed an unreasonable restraint on trade or the ability to conduct due diligence. In some jurisdictions, such absolute restrictions might be challenged as overly broad and against public policy, especially if it prevents proper legal or financial consultation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware Trade Secrets Act, 6 Del. C. § 2001
perturbation.law_explanation: The Delaware Trade Secrets Act, 6 Del. C. § 2001 et seq., defines misappropriation of trade secrets. While a confidentiality agreement strengthens the protection of trade secrets, an overly broad restriction with no exceptions (such as for legitimate business advisors) could be viewed unfavorably by courts. By removing the exception for disclosure to advisors, the clause potentially goes beyond what is reasonably necessary to protect legitimate trade secrets. Delaware courts balance the protection of trade secrets with the need for fair competition and freedom of information. A court might find that the overbroad restriction hinders legitimate business activities and is therefore unenforceable.

At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade was: A. Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; B. Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or C. Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not b
perturbation.justification: The modification removes an explicit exception allowing disclosure of confidential information to employees, agents, or advisors for evaluating a potential purchase. The Law Explanation argues this creates an overly broad restriction potentially hindering legitimate business activities, relating to the Delaware Trade Secrets Act. The scraped snippet defines "Misappropriation" as "Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent". By removing the clause that implies consent for disclosure to advisors, the modified text increases the risk of a court finding misappropriation, as it limits necessary disclosures. This strengthens the possibility of an overbroad restriction that a court might deem unenforceable, thus a contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: 1.6 shall, immediately upon written request fr om us, or without such request if you cease to be interested in purchasing the Business, return to us all written Confidential Information and expunge an y Confidential Information from any computer, word processor or other simil ar device in your possession or under your custody or control, without k eeping any copies, extracts or other reproductions thereof, and either return to us or destroy (such destruction to be certified in writing by you or a senior employee of yours who has supervised such destruction) all notes and memoran da prepared by you, your employees, agents or advisers, and any copies thereo f relating to any of the Confidential Information or the negotiations concerning the proposed sale of the Business;
perturbation.changed_text: 1.6 shall, immediately upon written request fr om us, or without such request if you cease to be interested in purchasing the Business, return to us all written Confidential Information and expunge an y Confidential Information from any computer, word processor or other simil ar device in your possession or under your custody or control, without k eeping any copies, extracts or other reproductions thereof, and either return to us or destroy all notes and memoran da prepared by you, your employees, agents or advisers, and any copies thereo f relating to any of the Confidential Information or the negotiations concerning the proposed sale of the Business;
perturbation.explanation: The original clause requires destruction of confidential information to be certified in writing. Removing this certification requirement makes it harder for the vendor to verify compliance, and harder for the recipient to prove compliance. This omission could be problematic if the recipient is based in a state with strong data security laws, as it weakens the documentation of their adherence to the confidentiality agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Massachusetts Standards for the Protection of Personal Information of Residents 201 CMR 17.00
perturbation.law_explanation: Massachusetts regulation 201 CMR 17.00 sets standards for the protection of personal information. While this agreement may not directly handle consumer data, it involves handling business information which may include personal data. Omitting the 'destruction to be certified in writing by you' weakens the documentation of compliance with data security standards. The Massachusetts regulation emphasizes the need for written policies and procedures to ensure data security and proper disposal of records containing personal information. The omission makes it harder to demonstrate reasonable measures were taken to protect such information, potentially leading to legal exposure if the recipient suffers a data breach.

perturbation.justification: The omission of the 'destruction to be certified in writing' requirement weakens the demonstration of compliance with data security standards as highlighted in the Law Explanation. The scraped snippet 1, referencing 201 CMR 17.00, establishes minimum standards to safeguard personal information. The original text required written certification of destruction, strengthening adherence to these standards. By removing this requirement, the changed text introduces a contradiction, as it diminishes the ability to prove compliance with data protection measures mandated by Massachusetts law.
